bugs.description: Servers bind to 0.0.0.0 (all interfaces) by default instead of 127.0.0.1. On Windows, a non-admin application binding to all interfaces triggers the Windows Defender Firewall prompt. Non-admin users cannot approve this prompt, causing immediate panic and often causing the server to be blocked.
bugs.reproduction_steps: Run iniciar.bat on a fresh Windows 10/11 machine with default Firewall rules and Standard User privileges.
bugs.fix: Explicitly bind to localhost. In Caddyfile, use 'localhost:18080' instead of ':18080'. In Python, use '--bind 127.0.0.1'. In Miniserve, use '--interfaces 127.0.0.1'.

bugs.description: Blind and dangerous taskkill cleanup. If python.pid is missing, the script loops through ALL processes listening on port 18081 and force-kills them. If the hospital has local telemetry or medical software on this port, it will be forcefully terminated.
bugs.reproduction_steps: Start an arbitrary process listening on 18081. Run 02-iniciar-python.bat, simulate a failed startup (or delete python.pid), and press a key to exit. The other process will be killed.
bugs.fix: Remove the netstat-based taskkill entirely. Instead, assign a unique window title using 'start "PythonAula"' and use 'taskkill /FI "WINDOWTITLE eq PythonAula"' or track the PID properly.

bugs.description: Health check heavily relies on powershell.exe. In many hospital environments, PowerShell is blocked for non-admins via AppLocker, Constrained Language Mode, or GPO. If blocked, the errorlevel is non-zero, the health check fails, and the browser never opens, even if the server actually started successfully.
bugs.reproduction_steps: Simulate AppLocker blocking powershell.exe or set ExecutionPolicy to Restricted (if it applies). Run iniciar.bat. The server starts, but the script reports FAILURE and directs the user to fallbacks.
bugs.fix: Use the native 'curl.exe' (available in Windows 10 1803+) for the health check. Example: curl.exe -s -f -o nul http://localhost:18080/metanalise/index.html. Fallback to just opening the browser blindly after 5 seconds if curl is unavailable.

race_conditions.description: PID capture occurs exactly 1 second after starting the executable. Windows Defender Antivirus aggressively intercepts new, unsigned executables (like caddy.exe) for real-time scanning on first launch. This often delays the server bind by 3 to 10 seconds. Netstat runs too early, finds nothing, and writes an empty/invalid caddy.pid. When the user exits, the cleanup fails, leaving Caddy as a background zombie process.
race_conditions.fix: Capture the PID dynamically inside the health-check loop once the server is actually confirmed to be responding on the port, rather than using a static 1-second timeout.

security_issues.description: By listening on :18080 (all network interfaces), the presentation (and potentially directory traversal depending on root config) is exposed to the entire hospital/corporate Local Area Network.
security_issues.attack_vector: A malicious actor on the hospital guest or internal Wi-Fi scans for open ports and accesses the unauthenticated medical data on the professor's laptop.
security_issues.fix: Change Caddyfile directive from ':18080' to 'localhost:18080' or '127.0.0.1:18080'.

ux_failures.scenario: Antivirus prompts or Firewall pops up during presentation setup.
ux_failures.what_user_sees: A yellow/red UAC prompt asking for Admin credentials, or a Windows Firewall block dialog.
ux_failures.what_user_expects: The presentation opens immediately like a PowerPoint file.
ux_failures.fix: Bind to 127.0.0.1 (prevents Firewall prompt). For SmartScreen/AV, digitally sign the binaries or wrap everything in a PyInstaller executable with a known clean signature, or instruct the user explicitly about the UAC prompt in the initial command window.

ux_failures.scenario: Professor attempts to run the presentation from inside the Windows Explorer ZIP viewer without extracting.
ux_failures.what_user_sees: Black window flashes, crashes, or fails to find relative paths because Windows extracts files to a temporary Temp/Rar$EXa directory piecemeal.
ux_failures.what_user_expects: The application handles the extraction or warns them clearly.
ux_failures.fix: Add a check at the top of the batch files: if '%~dp0' contains 'Temp' or 'AppData\Local\Temp', display a highly visible red/colored warning 'ERRO: VOCE PRECISA EXTRAIR O ZIP PRIMEIRO!' and PAUSE.

ux_failures.scenario: Professor right-clicks desbloquear.ps1 and selects 'Run with PowerShell', but enterprise GPO blocks it.
ux_failures.what_user_sees: Blue window flashes for a millisecond and closes. User thinks it worked, but files are still blocked (Mark of the Web), causing Caddy to fail again.
ux_failures.what_user_expects: A confirmation that files were unlocked or an explicit error.
ux_failures.fix: Provide a '00-desbloquear.bat' that wraps the PowerShell call with 'powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File desbloquear.ps1' and includes a 'pause' at the end so the professor can read the output.

missing_defenses.description: Validation of the current working directory preventing execution from UNC paths (e.g., \\server\share\aula). Command Prompt natively struggles with UNC paths and defaults to C:\Windows, breaking all relative paths.
missing_defenses.why_it_matters: If the professor loads the zip onto a hospital network drive instead of a USB stick, the batch script will silently fail to find the 'dist' folder and binaries.

missing_defenses.description: Graceful handling of stranded/zombie processes during initialization. The script checks if port 18080 is in use, and if so, completely aborts.
missing_defenses.why_it_matters: If the previous run crashed and orphaned the Caddy process, the non-technical professor has no idea how to 'Close black windows' as instructed, and the PID file might be missing. The script should automatically attempt to terminate orphaned caddy.exe processes before checking the port.

false_positives_to_skip: Lack of HTTPS/SSL certificates (Localhost-only environment doesn't require it).
Hardcoded ports (18080, 18081). Acceptable for local ephemeral servers.
Using taskkill /F instead of graceful shutdown (acceptable since these are read-only static file servers).
Missing authentication (acceptable as the service should only run on localhost).

overall_assessment.biggest_risk: The combination of binding to 0.0.0.0 (triggering Firewall admin prompts) and relying on PowerShell for health checks ensures this package will fail silently or loudly on tightly controlled hospital laptops.
top_3_fixes: Bind all servers explicitly to 127.0.0.1 (localhost) to avoid Windows Firewall admin prompts.
Replace the PowerShell Invoke-WebRequest health check with a native curl.exe check, with a fallback to just opening the browser blindly if curl fails.
Fix the race condition in PID capturing by checking netstat inside the 10-second wait loop, only capturing the PID AFTER the server has successfully bound to the port.
